Q1: I tried to keep things flexible because my schedule was pretty unpredictable. I'd usually carve out time in the evenings when I could listen to lectures or recorded materials since I learn best by hearing things explained. With 14 online courses going on, I couldn't be super rigid about it, so I'd prioritize based on what was most urgent that week. I aimed for around 15 hours of study time spread across the week, but honestly it shifted around a lot depending on what else was happening in my life.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and whatever else was going on. I'd have weeks where I was really consistent, logging in every day, and then other weeks where I'd barely touch anything until something was due. Being 25 and dealing with limited resources means sometimes I had to prioritize other things like work or personal stuff. But when I did sit down to study, I tried to make those sessions count even if they weren't happening at the same time each week.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or wanting to catch up on lecture recordings I'd missed. I'd get notifications about new content or upcoming assignments, and that would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd just feel motivated and want to get ahead, especially after a good CBT session when my mood was up. The discussion posts from classmates would also pull me in occasionally when I saw interesting conversations happening.
Q4: Yeah, there was a period mid-semester where logging in became almost automatic for me. I'd wake up, make coffee, and check the LMS before doing anything else. It happened because I realized I was falling behind on some courses and needed to stay on top of things. The habit stuck for a while, especially when I was feeling good mentally. Having those 24 peer interactions helped too because I wanted to stay connected with what others were discussing.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I'm an auditory learner. I watched over 147 minutes of video content because hearing explanations really helps things click for me. Discussion boards were useful too when people asked questions I hadn't thought of. I also appreciated when instructors recorded supplementary explanations because I could replay those parts I didn't understand the first time.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often skipped the reading-heavy PDFs and lengthy text documents. With my learning style, reading walls of text just doesn't stick as well as hearing someone explain it. I'd also delay the optional practice quizzes unless I really felt uncertain about material. Some of the supplementary resources felt redundant when the main lectures already covered everything I needed to know.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could tell it would directly help me understand something I was struggling with or if it counted toward my grade. I also valued anything I could listen to rather than read. If I could tell within the first few minutes that something was going to clarify a confusing concept, I'd stick with it. The time investment had to feel proportional to the benefit, especially when juggling so many courses.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. With required work from 14 courses, I had to be strategic about where I spent my energy. Required activities got done no matter what, even if I had to rush them. Optional stuff I'd only do if I was genuinely interested or if I had extra time, which honestly wasn't often. There was less guilt about skipping optional things, which made them feel lighter but also easier to ignore.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download lecture audio and listen while commuting or doing chores. I also used YouTube videos and podcasts to supplement concepts that weren't clicking from the course materials alone. Sometimes I'd find better explanations from external sources and spend hours with those instead. My notes app on my phone was full of voice memos I'd record summarizing what I learned.
Q10: There were many times I'd be walking around with my earbuds in, listening to downloaded lectures or my own recorded summaries, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also have long study sessions with a friend where we'd talk through concepts verbally, which is how I really cement my understanding. Group discussions in person or over the phone helped me more than typing in forums. The LMS couldn't capture any of that auditory processing I was doing.
Q11: All the time, yeah. I'd stream a video once to get the gist, then download the audio portion and listen to it multiple times throughout the week. One viewing in the LMS but maybe four or five listens offline. I did this with most of the core lecture content because repetition through listening is how I actually retain information. It was more efficient for me to study that way given my learning style.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially when stress got really high. Even though I generally feel happy and my mood has been good lately thanks to the CBT sessions, my resilience isn't great so setbacks hit hard. When multiple deadlines piled up across different courses, I'd feel overwhelmed and just shut down for a bit. Financial stress from having low resources also made it hard to focus sometimes because my mind would wander to those worries.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or had too much going on, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS just to stay afloat. Confusion about assignment requirements made me procrastinate because I didn't want to waste time doing something wrong. Competing responsibilities were huge since I'm managing a lot outside of school with limited resources. Those factors meant my LMS usage was inconsistent even when my actual learning effort was there.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be scrolling through materials but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd have a video playing but realize I hadn't absorbed anything because I was stressed about something else. Sometimes I'd log in just to feel like I was doing something productive, but I wasn't really engaged. Those sessions felt hollow and I'd have to redo them later when I could actually focus.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed by having 14 courses to manage. Sometimes I'd avoid starting something because I was afraid I wouldn't do it well enough. The CBT sessions helped me recognize this pattern, but breaking it is still a work in progress. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more ready, but that tomorrow kept moving.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my primary motivator for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become very active, watching videos I'd been putting off, checking discussion posts for hints. My login frequency definitely spiked around due dates. Without deadlines, I'd probably drift and focus on courses with more immediate pressure. It wasn't ideal but it's how I managed the workload across so many courses.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement. When I got positive feedback or a good grade, I felt motivated to keep going and would engage more with that course. Negative feedback sometimes made me avoid that course for a bit because it felt discouraging, though I knew I needed to address it. The timing mattered too - quick feedback kept me engaged, but delayed feedback made me lose momentum on that material.
Q18: The course structure really shaped how I participated. Courses with clear weekly modules and audio content kept me more engaged because I knew exactly what to do and could listen to it. When structure was loose or unclear, I'd fall behind because I wasn't sure what to prioritize. Having regular checkpoints helped me stay on track even when my attendance wasn't perfect. The structure gave me anchors in an otherwise chaotic schedule.
Q19: Courses that had good audio and video content made engagement way easier for me as an auditory learner. When everything was text-based, I struggled to stay focused and would disengage faster. Clear navigation and organized modules helped too. What made it harder was when instructions were vague or when there were too many small tasks that felt tedious. Also, courses that didn't allow downloading materials for offline use were frustrating.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying to figure things out but keeps hitting walls. They might be watching videos, reading discussions, searching for clarity, but still not understanding enough to complete the work confidently. Or maybe they're dealing with personal stuff that's eating up their mental energy. High activity could also mean they're anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually making progress. It doesn't always mean they're not trying.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me in some ways. I perform reasonably well but my LMS activity might look moderate because so much of my real learning happens offline through listening and verbal processing. Someone could download materials once and study them extensively without logging back in. Or they might have strong prior knowledge that means they don't need to engage with every resource. The LMS only shows one dimension of effort.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active, which resources you access, and whether you're meeting deadlines. That's useful data. But they miss the quality of engagement - whether you're actually learning or just clicking through. They can't capture offline studying, verbal discussions with peers, or the mental work happening when you're not logged in. They also can't show your emotional state or why your engagement might fluctuate.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material and it's sticking with me. It's not just going through the motions but genuinely trying to understand and connect concepts. For me, engagement often looks like listening to something multiple times, talking through ideas out loud, or explaining concepts to someone else. It's an active mental process, not just time spent in front of a screen. When I'm truly engaged, I feel curious and want to learn more.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I learn through listening and re-listening to audio offline. It wouldn't capture the conversations I have with peers outside the platform or the voice memos I record to process information. It also can't show the impact of my CBT sessions on my mood and motivation, or how financial stress sometimes pulls my attention away even when I want to focus. My engagement is deeper than what any log could measure.
